coredata.dc:title: Feasibility study of coffee husk char-derived carbon dots to enhance solar photovoltaic-thermal applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Analytical and Applied Pyrolysis
coredata.dc:creator: Suraj, P.
Sreekumar, Sreehari
Arun, P.
Muraleedharan, C.
coredata.dc:description: Coffee is one of the most traded commodities in the world. Coffee husk makes up the majority of the solid debris generated during the dry processing of coffee, which is between 30% and 50%. Pyrolysis is a reliable and feasible technology to valorise the coffee husk. The present study proposes and investigates a novel method for producing carbon dots from coffee husk char through pyrolysis. Nanomaterials are becoming more popular to improve the thermal and optical properties of working fluids used in solar photovoltaic and thermal conversion systems. The coffee husk-char derived carbon dot (CHC-CD) nanofluids are prepared with different char concentrations (1%, 2%, 3% and 4%). The chemical and optical properties of the nanofluid produced from coffee husk char (CHC-CD nanofluid) are investigated. The morphology, size and particle size distribution of the CHC-CD particles were studied using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The particles were found to have an average size of 5.38 nm. The CHC-CD nanofluid had a basic pH and negative zeta potential, which was found to decrease with an increase in the char concentration and pH. The nanofluid prepared with 2% char was found to have a zeta potential of −61.4 mV. It was evident from the UV–vis transmittance spectra that the CHC-CD nanofluid prepared with a char concentration of 2% had very limited transmittance in the UV region and good transmittance above 735 nm. This transmittance range is found to be favourable for the smooth and efficient operation of silicon-based solar PV cells. The CHC-CD nanofluid also showed a maximum intensity emission wavelength of 522 nm at an excitation wavelength of 450 nm. Characterisation of the carbon phase, surface functional groups, and surface defects was also done using XRD, FTIR, and Raman spectroscopy, respectively. The optical and chemical properties indicate that the developed CHC-CD nanofluid can find application not only in solar photovoltaic and thermal systems but also in applications in biomedical and chemical sensor applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Coffee is one of the most traded commodities in the world. Coffee husk makes up the majority of the solid debris generated during the dry processing of coffee, which is between 30% and 50%. Pyrolysis is a reliable and feasible technology to valorise the coffee husk. The present study proposes and investigates a novel method for producing carbon dots from coffee husk char through pyrolysis. Nanomaterials are becoming more popular to improve the thermal and optical properties of working fluids used in solar photovoltaic and thermal conversion systems. The coffee husk-char derived carbon dot (CHC-CD) nanofluids are prepared with different char concentrations (1%, 2%, 3% and 4%). The chemical and optical properties of the nanofluid produced from coffee husk char (CHC-CD nanofluid) are investigated. The morphology, size and particle size distribution of the CHC-CD particles were studied using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The particles were found to have an average size of 5.38 nm. The CHC-CD nanofluid had a basic pH and negative zeta potential, which was found to decrease with an increase in the char concentration and pH. The nanofluid prepared with 2% char was found to have a zeta potential of −61.4 mV. It was evident from the UV–vis transmittance spectra that the CHC-CD nanofluid prepared with a char concentration of 2% had very limited transmittance in the UV region and good transmittance above 735 nm. This transmittance range is found to be favourable for the smooth and efficient operation of silicon-based solar PV cells. The CHC-CD nanofluid also showed a maximum intensity emission wavelength of 522 nm at an excitation wavelength of 450 nm. Characterisation of the carbon phase, surface functional groups, and surface defects was also done using XRD, FTIR, and Raman spectroscopy, respectively. The optical and chemical properties indicate that the developed CHC-CD nanofluid can find application not only in solar photovoltaic and thermal systems but also in applications in biomedical and chemical sensor applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Solid waste management has become a significant concern worldwide, especially in industrial and agricultural sectors. Solid waste is usually disposed of in landfills, either open-fired or incinerated [1]. Finding potential applications for these solid wastes helps manage and prevent their adverse environmental effects. Coffee is a plantation crop [2], the second most traded commodity, and the beverage consumed most globally. It generates 30–50% solid waste during its dry processing [3–5]. Coffee husk is reported to have a high energy potential with a higher heating value of around 19.67 MJ/kg and is well-suitable for thermochemical conversion (gasification and pyrolysis) for energy extraction [6,7]. Thermo-chemical conversion, like pyrolysis, is a feasible, reliable, and efficient method for energy extraction and proper disposal of coffee husk. Coffee husk pyrolysis is an anoxic, high-temperature thermochemical process in which the coffee husk is heated to a temperature between 400 and 700 ℃ [8]. Coffee husk can be pyrolyzed at lower heating rates to produce biochar, a solid substance that increases the ability of soil to store water and nutrients while also helping in carbon sequestration [9]. The properties of biochar, such as adsorption capacity, high specific area, microporosity, etc., make it suitable for adsorption filters and catalysts [10]. Utilising the solid residue (char) obtained after extracting the volatiles and fuel gases for the synthesis of CD ensures maximum utilisation of the material which would be otherwise disposed of as solid waste.

In addition to the conventional applications of CH char, studies on the preparation of carbon dots (CD) are reported from pyrolysis. Among the top-down and bottom-up approaches for carbon dot preparation, the pyrolysis process falls under the bottom-up approach, in which small carbon units form CD under the application of thermal energy [11]. Generally, CDs have a spherical shape with particle sizes less than 20 nm [12,13]. The chemical stability, fluorescence behaviour, and low toxicity make CD suitable for chemical sensing, drug delivery, photocatalytic effects, and bioimaging. The most widely used bottom-up or one-step methods are microwave-assisted pyrolysis and hydrothermal methods [13]. A wide range of biomasses, including sweet potatoes, mint leaves, peanuts, chicken feathers, wheat straw, waste leather scrap, peanut shells, pineapple agro-waste, rice husk, mango leaves, coffee ground, and corn powder, were explored for their potential to be used in the hydrothermal process to create CD [14–21]. The feedstock goes through high-temperature dehydration and degradation as part of the pyrolysis process to create CD from biomass. The viability of producing CD via the pyrolysis process has previously been demonstrated for a variety of industrial wastes and biomasses, including fennel seed, coconut husk, watermelon peel, coffee grounds, and plant leaves [22] [23]. A study of the production of carbon dots from fennel seeds was also conducted using the pyrolysis method. The fennel seed was pyrolyzed at 500 ℃, then sonicated, centrifuged, and finally filtered using a dialysis bag [24]. Lychee seeds and peanut shells were pyrolyzed respectively at 300 ℃ and 250 ℃, then dispersed in DI water using ultrasound, centrifuged, and filtered to obtain CD [16,25]. In most of the above cases, biomass carbonization through pyrolysis was done at low temperatures (200–300 ℃) with some post-processing to obtain CD. The physical and chemical properties of green synthesised CDs make them suitable for different applications. The absorption band of CD was observed to fall in the range of 260–323 nm in most cases [26].

Apart from the application mentioned above, the CD synthesised by microwave-assisted heating was reported to be efficient in strong absorption of light less than 650 nm and greater than 1050 nm. This property of CD makes it feasible to use it as a spectral beam splitter/filter for PV/T systems [27]. PV cells respond only to a particular wavelength range of the solar spectrum. The rest of the irradiation induces heating and results in a rise in the temperature of the cell, which results in a decrease in efficiency. Air cooling, water cooling, and cooling assisted with phase change materials are the common methods used to reduce the operating temperature of PV modules [28–30]. The PVT system converts solar energy into electricity while simultaneously removing extra heat by dissipating it into a cooling fluid, increasing the total efficiency of the PV module. This additional heat can then be converted into thermal energy, which finds application mainly in hot water generation [31]. In a study, the maximum outlet temperature attained by the heat transfer fluid from the PVT system was 47 °C [32].

The spectral beam splitter finds application in solar PV cells as the PV cells respond to a particular wavelength range of solar irradiance. The rest of the spectral region induces heating and results in a rise in the temperature of the cell, which results in decreased efficiency. The operating range of solar PV cells depends on their band gap energy level. Silicon-based solar PV cells have an operating range of 700–1100 nm, whereas GaInP/GaAs can utilise lower wavelengths and have an operating range of 381–887 nm [33]. Efficiency decreases and temperature increases when light that was absorbed outside the operational range is converted to heat energy [34]. Thus, out of the total solar radiation that falls on the earth, those radiations with lower and higher wavelengths do not contribute to electrical power generation. The lower wavelength radiation possesses more energy, which ends up heating PV panels. Solar optical filters filter the radiation that falls outside the utilisation range of PV cells. Solid and liquid-based filters are being investigated for this purpose [28]. Liquid-based optical filters are efficient and economical, since they filter out unfavourable wavelengths to PV cells, enhancing the electrical output and generating thermal energy from it [35]. Looser et al. [36] investigated the optical transmittance of different commercial heat transfer fluids for application in a direct-absorption beam-splitting concentrated photovoltaic-thermal system. Results show that industrial-grade propylene glycol with ‘oil red inorganic dye’ was found to be best suitable. During the initial stage of research on solar optical filters, the feasibility of different fluids like water, glycol, inorganic aqueous salt, silicon oil, mineral oil, Brayco 888 HF, Therminol 66, Duratherm, Therminol VP-1, etc. as optical filter/spectral splitter for solar PVT systems had been studied theoretically and experimentally [37–40]. With the development of nanotechnology, different nanoparticles are developed and mixed with base fluids to enhance their optical properties and thus assist in the selective absorption and transmission of solar radiation [41,42]. Different nanoparticles can be used in various base fluid combinations to absorb or transmit a specific wavelength range. 73% of photovoltaic wavelength and 78% of non-photovoltaic wavelength got transmitted and absorbed, respectively, when silane-based heat transfer fluids were modified with indium tin oxide and gold nanoparticles were used as the fluid in the optical filter [43]. When comparing the effective spectral transmittance for various nanofluids, including water-ZnO, glycol-ZnO, water-Cu9S5, and water polypyrrole, it was discovered that water-ZnO performed most effectively [44]. The optical properties of nanofluids toward the absorption and transmission of selective band gap wavelength can be controlled by varying the size of nanoparticles, pH of the nanofluid, type of base fluid, concentration of particles, dopant etc. [43–45]. Other nanofluids that are feasible to be used as optical filters and help improve the thermal and electrical efficiency of solar PVT system are Ag/CoSO4, Au, Ag/TiO2, ITO, carbon nanotube, Al/water, Ag/water, Cu/water, etc. [46–51]. Although nanofluids increase the PVT system's overall efficiency, the expense of large-scale preparation and the complex technique prevent it from being economically feasible. These drawbacks of available nanofluids pave the way for developing cost-effective CD nanofluids. CD nanofluids are cost-effective, stable, easy to produce, possess good catalytic properties, exhibit solar absorption ability, suited for biomedical application etc. [52–54].

Though there are several studies reported on the synthesis of CDs, only limited works have been reported on the green synthesis of CD from pyrolysis. Coffee husk char, the solid residue obtained from pyrolysis process after extracting the fuel gases and volatiles is used as the precursor material. Synthesis of CD nanofluids from CH biomass waste can be considered an economical and efficient method as the pyrolysis process in the conversion process converts the waste CH into different value-added products. Thus, the synthesis of CD nanofluids from CH char aids in both the valorisation of CH and provides a value-added technical application in the form of an optical filter for solar PVT systems. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, CH is being used for the first time for the synthesis of CD and its feasibility to be used in solar photovoltaic-thermal applications has not been investigated before.

This study proposes a novel method for synthesising carbon dot (CD) nanofluids from coffee husk char. A preliminary feasibility analysis was conducted on the application of coffee husk char-derived carbon dot nanofluids in spectral-splitting PVT systems. The study involves the synthesis of coffee husk char through pyrolysis technique followed by downstream processes. The downstream processes involve ultrasonic dissolution, filtration, and centrifugation. The chemical, optical, and nanomaterial properties of the prepared nanofluid were investigated through various analyses, such as FTIR, XRD analysis, Raman spectroscopy, SEM analysis, zeta potential, UV–vis spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The synthesised CD nanofluid is cost-effective, stable and has good spectral absorption properties.

India is the seventh largest producer of coffee after leading producers like Brazil, Vietnam, Colombia, Indonesia, Ethiopia, and Honduras [55,56]. Coffee husk is thus available in plenty in the geographical region (Wayanad, Kerala, India) selected for material collection [57]. The current study uses the coffee husk produced during the dry processing (separation of the coffee bean from the husk) of coffee of the arabica variety. The coffee husk was collected after dehulling and dried in the open sun to remove moisture. It was then ground to a mean particle size of 0.6 mm. The dried and grounded coffee husk was golden brown in colour and it appears to be black in colour when charred. DI water was used as the base fluid to generate the CHC-CD nanofluid.

The grounded coffee husk (particle size less than 600
μ m
) was open sun-dried and 200 g of the sample was transferred to a batch-type pyrolyser. Using thermogravimetric analysis, the devolatilization zone of the coffee husk was determined to be between 200 and 500 °C. It was reported that the maximum weight loss happens in this zone and the maximum devolatilization temperature was identified 327 ℃
[58] . The pyrolysis temperature is optimised to 500 ℃ based on the quality and quantity of the char produced. Starting from the room temperature, the process temperature was increased to 500 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min and an inert atmosphere was maintained by admitting N2 gas at 3 L/min. A lower heating rate of 10
℃ / min
ensures a uniform heating of coffee husk, which ensures the quality of char with high carbon content [59]. The purging of inert gases, such as nitrogen, guarantees adequate pyrolysis by halting the oxidation of coffee husk and maximizing the amount of carbonaceous material produced. The coffee husk char thus formed (yield of 35%) was removed after the reactor was cooled to room temperature. Among the solid, liquid and gaseous products formed during pyrolysis, this study focuses on the solid char product. The schematic of the experimental setup is shown in Fig. 1.

The reactor is made up of a detachable top that is fitted with ports with controllers for the extraction of volatile materials and the purging of nitrogen, respectively. The band type heater used ensures a uniform heating of the reactor, preventing localised heating. Despite the fact that coffee husk char has a variety of uses, the current work explores the viability of using it as a source of CD to create CD nanofluids. The char thus formed was then mixed thoroughly with DI water at different proportions (1%, 2%, 3% and 4%), soaked and kept undisturbed for 6 hours. The mixture was then sonicated using an ultrasonicator for 1 hour. A homogenized mixture was obtained at the end of the sonication process, which was then filtered using a qualitative filter paper. The filtering process separates the solid and liquid components from the mixture, and a black fluid was obtained as the filtrate. The black fluid was then centrifuged for 10 minutes at 5000 rpm. The supernatant solution was taken for further analysis and characterization. The synthesis process of CH-char-derived carbon dot fluid (CHC-CD) and its fluorescence when exposed to UV light is shown in Fig. 2.

To establish the optical characteristics and to decide the suitability of CHC-CD nanofluids for solar PVT applications and other purposes, chemical and optical characterizations of the nanofluids are necessary.

The proximate and ultimate analysis was conducted to determine the gross composition (moisture, fixed carbon, volatile matter and ash content) and elemental composition of CHC (percentage composition of C, H, N, S and O). The analysis was carried out on the basis of ASTM standards. The higher heating value of char was determined using a bomb calorimeter as per ASTM standard D-2015.

The morphology of the CD fluid was determined by field emission scanning electron microscope analysis (ZEISS Gemini SEM 300). The SEM images are obtained at nano scales of 200 and 20 nm. The particle structure, distribution and mean particle size were analysed using an HRTEM image using (High-resolution transmission electron microscopy) HRTEM: Jeol/JEM 2100 equipment. The sample's X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was done using a PANalytical X′Pert3 Powder X-Ray Diffractometer. Cu-K α radiation of 1.5410 Å was used as the X-ray source with 2 θ scanning mode ranging from 10 to 80 deg. The elemental composition was determined, with the aid of EDX analysis to ensure the presence of different compounds detected in XRD analysis. The data was obtained using Oxford instruments, X-acta (PentaFET Precision). The stability of nanofluids plays a crucial role in ensuring uniformity and consistency. The zeta potential was determined using a particle charge reader (Microtrac Europe GmbH, ZETA-Check). As the surface charges or stability of the nanofluids depends on the pH of the solution, it was determined using a digital pH meter (EUTECH Instruments PC 700) at different concentrations. In addition to it, the stability of the CHC-CD nanofluid was also determined by a centrifuge assisted UV-Vis spectroscopy. The presence of functional groups and their chemical bonding in CD can be determined using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The analysis was carried out on a Jasco FT/IR-4700 spectrometer. The attenuated total reflectance (ATR) method was followed and a wavenumber range of 400–4000 cm −1 was used for scanning. The Raman spectra of the sample were also obtained using a Raman spectrometer (LabRAM HR, HORIBA) with an excitation source of 532 nm using a 100× objective lens. A laser power less than 5 mW (100 mW power with 5% ND filter) was used with an acquisition time of 2 s and accumulation of 2, and the data were acquired in a range of 190–4000 cm−1.

The optical properties, stability and photoluminescence of the prepared CHC-CD nanofluid need to be determined to investigate its feasibility as an optical filter and absorption fluid for solar PVT applications. The optical properties of CHC-CD nanofluid can be explored by conducting ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV-Vis spectroscopy was performed using Peak Instruments C7200 UV-Vis spectrophotometer, in a range of 190–1100 nm with a wavelength interval of 1 nm. The optical transmittance of the nanofluid was measured using Beer Lambert law as shown in Eq. (1) [60].

The solar weighted absorption percentage of the nanofluid was calculated to effectively analyse the radiation absorption potential of the fluid. Solar-weighted absorption fraction/percentage (SWAF) indicates the amount of solar radiation that could be absorbed by the fluid while the beam travels through the medium [61]. UV-Vis-NIR spectroscopic data of nanofluids was used for calculating SWAF, as shown in Eq. (2).

represents the ASTM G-173 reference solar spectrum at AM 1.5 standard [62–65]. The extinction coefficient was calculated, using Beer Lambert’s law, from the transmittance spectra of nanofluids. The penetration depth is the thickness of the cuvette (10 mm).

Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (CARY ECLIPSE, VARIAN). The excitation wavelength was varied from 370 to 500 nm and the emission peaks were plotted. Determining the physical, chemical, and optical characteristics of char and CHC-CD fluid enables understanding its feasibility for solar PVT applications. The investigated characteristics also envisage the possibility of other value-added applications.

The synthesis of CD from CH char through the pyrolysis process is an economical and environment-friendly method. Compared to the traditional way of CD preparation, the synthesis process is more viable and doesn't call for additional expensive ingredients or reagents. Only after a thorough characteristic study of its chemical and optical properties it will be possible to determine whether this CHC-CD nanofluid is suitable for use as an optical filter, a thermal energy absorber, and other applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of coffee husk and coffee husk-char
ce:section.ce:para: The CH obtained after dry processing of coffee appears to be golden brown when grounded to a small size. A previous study has reported the thermogravimetric analysis of coffee husks [6] and identified the different zones of mass degradation. The identified zones are tabulated in Table 1. As the TGA analysis showed that the highest devolatilization occurs below 500 °C, the pyrolysis temperature was selected the same. At 500 °C, the maximum amount of gaseous and other condensable materials can be extracted from coffee husk.

The properties and application of the black-coloured char depend on its chemical composition. The proximate and ultimate analysis are carried out based on dry air basis and dry ash-free basis, respectively and the results are tabulated in Table 2. The carbon percentage from the ultimate analysis represents the total carbon available in the elemental form, whereas the fixed carbon from proximate analysis denotes the left-over carbon after all the volatiles were burned off by heating it at 950 °C in the absence of air. The char thus formed, which was rich in carbon, was used for the preparation of CD nanofluid.

The morphology of the CHC-generated CD nanoparticles was studied using SFE-SEM and HR-TEM analysis. The Fig. 3 depicts the SEM image of CHC-CD nanoparticles at different nanoscales of 200 and 20 nm. The spherical shape of the particles was evident from the SEM image and the particles appeared to be distributed and agglomerated. The obtained morphology resembles the structure of nitrogen and Sulphur dopped CDs [66].

Fig. 4 (a) shows the TEM image of CHC-CDs at a resolution of 20 nm and given its particle size distribution is given in Fig. 4 (b). The particles appeared to be ostensibly spherical and agglomerated. The average size of the particle and the particle size distribution was analysed using ImageJ software. The image was segmented and the length/diameter of individual selected particle was determined with the aid of ImageJ as shown (red lines) in Fig. 4 (a). The particle size of 200 such particles is found out individually and represented the particle size distribution using a histogram as shown in Fig. 4 (b). The particle size distribution was observed to be in the range of 2.6 nm - 9.5 nm with an average size of 5.38 nm. The particle size determined from TEM were reported to be comparable with that of the particle size obtained from DLS analysis [67]. Similar particle sizes were also reported in other studies on green synthesised carbon dots [24,68]. Fig. 4 (c) and (d) show the high-resolution image at 10 nm and 2 nm respectively. The inner core of the particle resembled to have a crystalline structure [69]. The irregularity in the structure of the agglomerates may be due to random mutual fusing that might have happened during synthesis.

The XRD pattern of the CHC-CD was obtained by coating the CHC-CD nanofluid on a glass slide and allowing it to dry. The pattern obtained had several peaks with major ones at 2 θ values of 12°, 16.84°, 24.21°, 28.29°, 33.72°, 34.05°, 34.391°, 39.2°, 40.44°, 42.12°, 44.45°, 48.79°, 49.27
° , 50.10 °
and 52.15
° .
The XRD pattern of the sample is shown in Fig. 5. The characteristics peaks at 12°, 24.21°, 34.05°, 34.391°, 39.20°, 40.44°, 44.45°, 48.79°, 49.27° and 52.15° correspond to potassium bicarbonate. The peaks observed at 16.84° correspond to 002 planes of carbon (Alarfaj et al. observed a peak at 19.18°) representing the 002 planes of the CD [70]. A small shift in the peak was also reported [71,72]. The peak at 42.21° is comparable to cubic carbon, while the peaks at 28.29° and 50.10° are similar to cyano groups, confirming the possibility of the formation of carbon-nitrogen bonds. Thus, the structure of the formed CHC-CD resembles to carbon nitride phase structure. The peak corresponding to potassium bicarbonate was observed as the char of the coffee husk was usually rich in alkali elements such as potassium [6].

Th EDX spectra of the CHC-CD nanoparticles were obtained by drying the fluid in a hot air oven at a temperature of 70 ℃ and then taken for EDX analysis. The EDX spectrum revealed the presence of different elements and their percentage composition. The spectrum is shown in Fig. 6 and corresponding percentage composition is tabulated in Table 3. From the EDX data, it is evident that the synthesised CHC-CD nanoparticles has carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and potassium as its major composition. Other trace elements present altogether contributes to only 1.8%. The composition of the precursor material (CH) contributes to the presence of other elements like oxygen and potassium. The percentage composition of oxygen and potassium in CH was found to be 43.64% and 4.25%, respectively [58]. The EDX composition confirms the presence of compounds observed in the XRD spectrum given in Fig. 5. The possibility of the removal of the traces of residual precursor material using dialysis process was also noted [73,74].

The FTIR analysis determines the surface functional groups associated with the synthesized CHC-CD. The CD nanofluid was evaporated to obtain the solid left over, which was collected and analysed. The percentage transmittance was plotted against wavenumber (4000–500 cm−1) and shown in Fig. 7. The spectra show a broad absorption between 3694 and 2640 cm−1 with a peak value at 3145 cm−1. According to the literature, the stretching vibrations of the OH and NH functional groups are responsible for the band between 3100 and 3300. The NH and OH functional groups play an important role in ensuring the stability and hydrophilicity of CD in an aqueous solution. The peak at 1646 represents the presence of the carbonyl group and it also shows the presence of NH2 through amide bonds. The presence of nitrogen is expected as purging of nitrogen was done during the char preparation. The peaks at 1646, 1360 and 1113 are attributed to the vibrations of C-O-C bonds [75], stretching of CH/CN [76] and stretching of C-O [77], respectively. The presence of aromatic rings with sp2 C-H bending patterns is reckoned between 900 and 612 cm−1. Thus, the FTIR spectra of CHC-CD particles confirmed the presence of hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amino groups. However, upon comparing the FTIR spectra of DI water and CHC-CD nanofluid, it was discovered that the spectra of the two fluids overlapped, as illustrated in Fig. S1 . It is presumed that the deprivation of the peaks may be due to the presence of DI water in higher concentration than the particles when in fluid state and there are chances for peaks of water overshadows the other signals. Similar FTIR peaks of CDs were reported in literature [78–80].

Raman spectroscopy produces a unique signature for different molecules and molecular structures. The Raman spectrum of CD depends on its composition, structure, surface functional groups, defects etc. CHC-CD nanofluid was drop casted on a glass plate followed by drying in a hot air oven maintained at 70 ℃ to obtain a thin film and the CHC-CD Raman spectra of this thin film was obtained. Fig. 8 displays the Raman spectra of CHC-CD nanoparticles obtained with a 532 nm excitation laser with an acquisition time of 2 s and accumulation of 2. The peaks were observed around 1350 and 1580 cm−1 and it represented the D-band and G-band, respectively. The peaks observed were found to be matching with CD synthesised from fennel seed through a similar process [81]. Similar characteristic peaks are also reported in other works [82–84]. The D-band is attributed to defects of carbon lattice or disorder ness of sp3 carbon and the G-band corresponds to the sp2 hybridised carbon. A similar observation was made by Genc et al. [85]. The intensity of the D-band and G-band are represented as
I
D
and
I
G
respectively, and the
I
D
I
G
value indicates the defect in the sp2 carbon lattice of CD and higher value represents the presence of oxygenated functional groups [86–88]. The CHC-CD has an
I
D
I
G
value of 0.86, which denotes good graphitization of CHC-CD. The graphene quantum dots with lower
I
D
I
G
values (0.45, 0.8 etc.) were reported to have good structural order [89] and the material with an
I
D
I
G
value less than one was reported to have good graphitization [90,91]. Hence, the synthesised CHC-CD is expected to have good graphitization as well as good structural order. A full-range Raman spectrum of CHC-CD is given in Fig. S2 , which had another peak located at 2900 cm−1. This discernible peak in the Raman spectra is attributed to the CH bond [92] and the existence of CH bending vibrations are evident from peaks at 1360 cm−1, 843 cm−1 and 615 cm−1 of the FTIR spectra given in Fig. 7 [81,93–96] .

Knowing the optical properties of the CHC-CD is very significant in understanding its feasibility for use as a selective spectral absorber or beam splitter/optical filter. The fluid appears to be brown topaz in colour under daylight. The colour appears to be stable and consistent even after days of storage. Fig. 9 (a) represents the appearance of DI water and the CHC-CD nanofluids under visible light and Fig. 9 (b) shows its appearance under UV light.

Filtering out the unwanted wavelengths of solar radiation helps in decreasing the solar PV cell temperature and extracting energy associated with these wavelengths through absorption fluid helps in improving the overall efficiency of the system. The silicon PV cells use only visible and some parts of IR to generate electricity. So, the expected property of the developed nanofluid is such that it should also be a good absorber of UV (200–380 nm) and some of the IR. The present study investigates its optical properties using UV-Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy under room temperature. Fig. 10 illustrates the UV–visible absorption spectra of the CHC-CD nanofluid. A broad absorption range was observed with two peaks at 250 nm and 323 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra. The peak at 250 nm represents the π−π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain from the carbon core of CHC-CD and the peak at 323 nm represents the n-π* transition of CO bonds [24,97,98]. The broad absorption range is due to the random energy levels of CD and its complex band structure. A similar absorption band centered at 272 nm and 320 nm was observed in the UV-Vis spectra of hydrophilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots synthesized from dwarf banana peel [90].

The transmittance spectra of a CHC-CD nanofluid utilizing DI water as the base fluid are shown in Fig. 11 at different concentrations. The transmittance is an important optical property with which the feasibility of the fluid to be used as an optical filter can be verified. The transmittance spectra of DI water show maximum transmittance to almost all the incident wavelength ranges (190–1100 nm). The transmittance spectra of CHC-CD nanofluids vary as the concentration changes. The UV–vis spectrum of the CHC-CD nanofluids varies such that it shows zero transmittance at lower wavelengths and reaches its maximum possible value (60–80%) at a wavelength of 735 nm and beyond. Si PV cells are stated to have an operating range between 751 and 1126 nm for both its short and long wavelength response edges [99]. The transmittance range of CHC-CD nanofluids is observed to be most suited for use in this range The transmittance was found to decrease with the increase in the concentration of char used during the preparation of the nanofluid. The CHC-CD nanofluids with char concentrations of 2% and above cut out the lower wavelength rays in the region of 190–450 nm, which falls almost in the UV range. As the transmittance was compared, it was determined that the CHC-CD nanofluid made from 2% char (CHC-CD2) is the most suitable because it not only filters out lower wavelength radiation but also has a transmittance of roughly 70% in the operating range, which was higher than that of CHC-CD3 and CHC-CD4 nanofluids. The transmittance of CHC-CD2 nanofluid was found to be 48.62% less than that of DI water at a wavelength of 680 nm and 8% at 920 nm. Beyond 920 nm, it has been noted that the transmittance of both DI water and CHC-CD2 nanofluids exhibits nearly identical variation. Therefore, the CHC-CD2 nanofluid was found to serve the purpose of blocking UV and near-IR spectra and has better transmittance in the operating range of PV cells. The color change of the fluid in the presence UV light denotes the fluorescence (FL) property of the CHC-CD nanofluid.

Spectral absorption spectra of nanofluids and base fluid in the wavelength of 280 – 1100 nm are shown in Fig. 12. The absorption spectra are compared with the reference solar spectrum at AM 1.5 to study the spectral-specific absorption pattern of the fluids. An absorption spectrum coinciding with the reference indicates that the beam entering the fluid volume was completely absorbed without any loss. All the nanofluids showed similar absorption patterns along the wavelength range, as observed in their transmittance spectrum. It could be observed from the spectrum that an increase in the weight fraction of the nanoparticles enhances absorption. A steep rise in absorption was observed for nanofluids in the spectral range of 300 – 450 nm. And the absorption peak was observed to lie in the range of 420 – 480 nm. The nanofluid with a weight fraction of 4%, exhibited the highest absorption along the spectrum. The highest value for SWAF of around 80.19% was observed for this nanofluid concentration. Other nanofluids showed absorption characteristics in the decreasing order of their concentration. The SWAF of nanofluids with 3, 2 and 1 wt% were 72.88, 63.59, and 57.10%, respectively. SWAF of DI water was recorded to be the lowest by around 12.3%. Therefore, the nanofluid with 4 wt% was found to be the optically efficient fluid with an enhancement of 67.89% over the base fluid.

To investigate the emission characteristics of CHC-CD nanofluid, the excitation-based emission spectra are plotted after conducting fluorescence spectroscopy. The fluorescence emission spectra are obtained by exiting the fluid imperceptibly from 380 nm to 500 nm with an interval of 10 nm. Fig. 13 depicts the fluorescence emission spectra of CHC-CD nanofluid. With an increase in the excitation wavelength from 380–500 nm, emission peaks are found to be red-shifted from 474 to 550 nm. According to the literature, the size of the CD particles and the presence of surface functional groups, defects, and band gaps are the causes of the red shift [100]. The highest fluorescence intensity was observed to be at 522 nm, which corresponds to an excitation wavelength of 450 nm, resulting in green fluorescence. The presence of a significant number of particles being excited at this wavelength is what causes the phenomena of green emission at 522 nm at an excitation wavelength of 450 nm.

The zeta potential of the CHC-CD was evaluated at different concentrations (1–4%), and the pH was also found to vary with the concentration of the CHC-CD. The pH of the CHC-CD nanofluid was found to be basic in nature and was found to increase with an increase in concentration. The pH value increased from 11.3 to 11.9 as the char concentration increased from 1% to 4%. Fig. 14 shows the variations of zeta potential and pH with concentration. The development of zeta potential is a result of different factors involving the loss of ions from the crystal structure, the adsorption of charged species from the solution, and the ionization of chemical groups on the surface [101]. The zeta potential also depends on the pH, concentration of the solution, volume fraction, and particle size [102–104]. The nanofluids with a high zeta potential are more stable, because the charges on the particles stabilise and prevent coalescence. The zeta potential value outside the range of ± 30 mV was reported to be more stable [105,106]. Fig. 14 shows that the Zeta potential varies from −66.57 mV to −39.3 mV as the concentration is varied from 1% to 4%. The CHC-CD nanofluids at all of the concentrations considered are stable and were found to be more stable at lower concentrations. Negative zeta potential was found to decrease with the increase in pH and the pH was found to increase with the increase in concentration. The increase in char concentration also produces an increase in ionic strength. The basic CD is found to be stable at low ionic concentration since the rise in ionic concentration reduces the thickness of electrical double layer [107].

Though the zeta potential is a proven method for finding the synthesis stability of the nanofluid, the centrifugation-assisted UV-Vis spectroscopy was reported to be more efficient [108]. The CHC-CD nanofluid was centrifuged for 30 min at an 8000-rpm followed by UV-Vis spectroscopy. The change in transmittance and decrease in the transmittance of CHC-CD nanofluid at different concentration before centrifugation (BC) and after centrifugation (AC) are compared as shown in Fig. 15.

From Fig. 15, it is evident that the maximum decrease in the average transmittance was found to be less than 11%. The transmittance change had a similar trend at each concentration but a change in value was observed. The decrease in the percentage transmittance was found to be low (7.3%) in case of CHC-CD2 fluid which was the lowest among the fluids at other tested concentrations. Thus, the stability of CHC-CD nanofluid supports its suitability for application as an optical filter in solar PV systems. The transmittance spectra of CHC-CD2 before and after performing centrifugation is shown in Fig S3. A qualitative stability analysis of the synthesised fluid was conducted by visually inspecting the change in the physical appearance of the fluid. 25 ml of CHC-CD2 nanofluid was taken in a small glass bottle and left undisturbed for about 12days. The appearance of the fluid at the beginning and end of the period is shown in Fig S4. The physical appearance of the fluid was noted and only a very negligible amount of particle settling was observed.

The study has exemplified an economical and simple approach to synthesising CD nanofluids through pyrolysis. The coffee husk waste was used as the carbon source to synthesise CD. The investigation has proved the ability of CHC-CD nanofluid to be used as an optical filter for solar PV cells and that it could also be employed for solar thermal applications due to its ability to absorb lower wavelengths. The FESEM analysis of CHC-CD nanofluids revealed the formation of aggregates and the agglomerated morphology of CHC-CD particles in DI water. The particle distribution was revealed through HRTEM analysis and the mean particle size was determined to be 5.38 nm. The phase structure was revealed by XRD analysis and the peak corresponding to the carbon 002 plane was observed at 16.84° and the formation of nitrogen bonds was also confirmed. The pH of the prepared fluid was found to be basic, and it increased (11.3–11.9) with the increase in concentration. The CHC-CD nanofluid was shown to be stable, with a maximum zeta potential value of −66.57 mV and the stability of CHC-CD2 nanofluid was observed with an average decrease of transmittance of only 7.3% when centrifuged at 8000 rpm. The FTIR spectra revealed the presence of different functional groups like hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amino groups. The
I
D
I
G
value obtained from Raman spectroscopy (around 0.86) denotes good structural order and graphitisation of the synthesised material. The CHC-CD2 nanofluid was found to be the most suitable among the fluids prepared with different char concentrations. The UV–vis absorption spectra were similar to those of previous work, and the transmittance spectra showed zero or no transmittance in the UV region, whereas they showed good transmittance in the range beyond 735 nm, which is required for the smooth and efficient operation of a silicon-based solar PV cell. Thus, the research recommends using CHC-CD nanofluid in solar PVT applications. The future scope of the work includes the further purification of the CHC-CD nanofluid and its real-time testing in laboratory-scale setups as an optical filter, beam splitter, and absorption fluid.
